title: Run your first program
instruction:
This is a Python program. It has exactly one line.

`print()` tells Python to display something on the screen. Whatever you put inside the quotes gets printed.

Just run it.
successMessage: Hello, World! — the first program every programmer ever writes. You are officially a programmer now.

title: Make it say your name
instruction:
Change `"Hello, World!"` to `"Hello, "` followed by your own name.

Example: `print("Hello, Alex!")`
hint: Change the text inside the quotes. Keep the quotes — they tell Python this is text.

codeChecks.message: Change the message — replace World with your own name.

successMessage: Your name on screen. The program is already yours.

title: Print two lines
instruction:
Add a second `print()` below the first one.

Each `print()` starts a new line automatically.

Make the second line say something about what you do — like `"I am learning Python."`
hint: Add print("I am learning Python.") on the next line.
starter:
print("Hello, Alex!")
# Add a second print() below this line

codeChecks.message: You need two print() calls — one per line.
Replace the comment with your second print() call.

successMessage: Two lines of output, two lines of code. Python runs top to bottom — each print() adds one line.

title: Print three lines
instruction:
Add a third `print()` below the other two.

Make it say where you are from — like `"From London."`
hint: Add a third print() below the second one.
starter:
print("Hello, Alex!")
print("I am learning Python.")
# Add a third print() below

codeChecks.message: Replace the comment with your third print() call.

successMessage: Three lines. You now know the most-used function in Python. Seriously — you will call print() thousands of times.

title: Comments
instruction:
A comment starts with `#`. Python ignores everything after `#` on that line.

Comments are notes for humans — not instructions for Python.

Run this. The comment does nothing. Then change the comment to describe what the program does.
hint: Change the comment text to anything you like. It won't affect the output.
starter:
# This is a comment — Python ignores this line
print("Hello, Python!")
print("Comments are just notes for humans.")
expectedOutput: Comments are just notes for humans.
successMessage: Comments never run. They exist for you — and anyone else reading your code. Good programmers comment the 'why', not the 'what'.

title: No main, no semicolons
instruction:
In Java or C++, every program needs a `main` function and semicolons at the end of every line.

Python needs neither. Code at the top level just runs, top to bottom.

Run this program — no `main`, no `;`. Then add two more `print()` lines about Python.
hint: Add two print() lines below the existing ones. No semicolons needed.
starter:
print("Python runs top to bottom.")
print("No main function needed.")
# Add two more lines below

codeChecks.message: Add two more print() calls — four total.
Replace the comment with your two print() calls.

successMessage: Four lines, zero semicolons, zero curly braces. Python gets out of your way and lets you focus on what you want to say.

title: Spot the mistake
instruction:
This program has a bug. The string is not closed properly — there is a missing quote.

Find it and fix it.
hint: The closing quote is missing from the first print. It should be print("Python is great!")
starter:
print("Python is great!)
print("Let's fix this bug.")

codeChecks.message: Fix the missing closing quote: print("Python is great!")

successMessage: Syntax errors are the most common bugs for beginners. The fix was one character — a closing quote. Your eye for bugs is already sharpening.

title: Another bug to fix
instruction:
Python is case-sensitive. `Print` is not the same as `print`.

`Print("Hello")` will crash. `print("Hello")` works.

Fix both lines.
hint: Change Print to print (lowercase p) on both lines.
starter:
Print("Hello from Python!")
Print("Case matters here.")

codeChecks.message: Change Print to print — Python is case-sensitive.

successMessage: print not Print. Lowercase always. Python's built-in functions are all lowercase — this will become automatic very quickly.

title: Write a program from scratch
instruction:
The editor is empty. Write a program yourself.

Print exactly these three lines:
```
Python is fun.
I wrote this myself.
Chapter 1 almost done.
```
hint: Three print() calls, one per line.

codeChecks.message: Use print() to output each line.

successMessage: You built that from nothing. Three print() calls, three lines of output, exactly right. That is programming.

title: Chapter 1 complete
instruction:
One last change.

The program below prints `"Learning Python"`. Change it to print `"Chapter 1 complete!"`
hint: Change the text inside the quotes.

codeChecks.message: Change the message to: Chapter 1 complete!

successMessage:
Chapter 1 complete!

You know print(), comments, basic syntax, and how to find bugs. Everything else in Python builds from here.
